role_summary: Owner of Jean's Beauty Shop at 510 Mobile Street in Hattiesburg, Mississippi; co-founder (April 1964) and Executive Secretary of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP); Treasurer of the 1964 Freedom Summer project in Hattiesburg; member of the August 1964 MFDP delegation to the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City.
ai_reading: Cosine similarity in this corpus puts Connor's testimony closest to Charles McLaurin's (entry 17) at an aggregate count of 211, the strongest single-neighbor pull on her page, though her second tier is the more telling pattern: a dense Hattiesburg-and-Forrest-County cluster of Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92, count 141), the joint Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins interview (entry 46, count 116), Rosie Head (entry 111), Walter Bruce (entry 123), Leesco Guster (entry 77), and Juadine Henderson (entry 68, whose top passage scores the highest cosine of the set at 0.739). The embedding reads her less as one of the nationally-catalogued MFDP delegates and more as a node in a local Hattiesburg testimony network whose shared vocabulary is the COFO office, the citizenship class, and the Mobile Street block. Two axis positions track the public framing of a precinct organizer and beauty-shop owner: on the Tactical Pragmatism versus Strategic Vision axis the model places her at +0.62 (normalized) toward Tactical Pragmatism, the hands-on pole her precinct-by-precinct, poll-tax-by-poll-tax method would predict; and on the Southern Struggle versus Northern Struggle axis she reads at -0.23 toward the Southern pole, consistent with a lifetime in one Mississippi city, though the modest size of that southern lean (rather than a stronger reading) may track her testimony's heavy attention to the national convention in Atlantic City and the federal-court reapportionment fight in Connor v. Johnson alongside the Forrest County fieldwork. The novel reading her page surfaces is the local-business-owner-as-infrastructure signature: her beauty shop sat across the street from the COFO office on Mobile Street, and the embedding ties her to the other Hattiesburg voices through that block rather than through the MFDP's state or national leadership.
biographical_paragraph: Peggy Jean Connor's Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript. Connor's external biographical record is well-documented in the Mississippi Encyclopedia and the SNCC Digital Gateway [src: 1, src: 2]. Connor was born October 29, 1932 to John Henry and Esther Gould in Hattiesburg, Mississippi's Mobile Street community, the city's historically African American district. She attended Eureka High School, the city's second modern brick school for African American students. Connor owned Jean's Beauty Shop at 510 Mobile Street, and after SNCC organizers established an office near her business she became one of the central Hattiesburg organizers [src: 2]. In April 1964 she was a founding member of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and served as chairperson of her precinct (the "library precinct" in Hattiesburg) and across the project as Executive Secretary of the MFDP [src: 2]. From June through August 1964 she served as an officer for the Hattiesburg chapter of the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) and as Treasurer of the Hattiesburg Freedom Summer project; she joined the MFDP delegation to the August 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City and was among the delegates who refused the two-seat compromise the Johnson administration offered the MFDP, returning under Klan threats and intimidation [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Peggy Jean Connor
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked when she first tried to register to vote in Forrest County, Connor describes the years of repeated attempts under the registrar Theron Lynd:
interview_snippets.quote: It took me about 10 years to become registered.

interview_snippets.speaker: Peggy Jean Connor
interview_snippets.lead_in: Before she had joined any organization, Connor was using her shop as a registration aid, paying the poll tax for customers who were afraid to go to the courthouse themselves:
interview_snippets.quote: Poll tax. And I would pay poll tax. And I would collect poll tax in my beauty shop for patrons that wanted, and it was afraid to go themselves.

interview_snippets.speaker: Peggy Jean Connor
interview_snippets.lead_in: Connor places the movement's Hattiesburg headquarters in relation to her own business on Mobile Street (the transcript renders the Council of Federated Organizations as "co-pho"):
interview_snippets.quote: The co-pho office was across the street from my beauty shop.

interview_snippets.speaker: Peggy Jean Connor
interview_snippets.lead_in: She recalls how she was drawn into the organizing she had been watching from across the street:
interview_snippets.quote: And I was standing in the door of the beauty shop. And I had been seeing a lot of commotion going on over there.

interview_snippets.speaker: Peggy Jean Connor
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the MFDP delegation's refusal of the two-seat compromise at the 1964 Atlantic City convention, Connor describes a split within the delegation:
interview_snippets.quote: But, you know, to tell the truth, the men in the group wanted it. It was the women that saved us.

movement_context: Hattiesburg held a particular weight in Freedom Summer 1964: of the four congressional-district COFO project bases the summer organizers established (Jackson, Greenville, Meridian, Hattiesburg), the Hattiesburg project was the largest by volunteer count and by enrollment in the parallel Freedom Schools network, with the city's Forrest County voter-registration drive serving as the COFO movement's marquee local test of the federal voting-rights pressure that the summer was designed to generate [src: 3, src: 4]. The infrastructure that absorbed the influx of out-of-state volunteers and routed them into door-to-door registration work was a community-business network anchored on Mobile Street, the city's historically African American commercial corridor a few blocks east of the Illinois Central Railroad tracks. Connor's beauty shop at 510 Mobile Street was one of the visible nodes in that network, along with the True Light Baptist and St. John United Methodist church basements that hosted Freedom Schools and the Smith Drug Store and Holmes Club that served as informal meeting spaces [src: 3, src: 5]. The catalog's other clear small-business-owner-as-organizer page is Barbara Edna Vickers (entry 5), the St. Augustine, Florida beautician whose shop occupied a structurally analogous position in the 1963 to 1964 St. Augustine SCLC direct-action campaign that drew Martin Luther King Jr. to the city; the Connor and Vickers pages together establish the small-business-as-organizing-infrastructure pattern as a cross-regional structural feature rather than a Hattiesburg or St. Augustine particular [src: 3, src: 4]. After Freedom Summer ended, the MFDP precinct organizing pivoted to a sustained local-government and federal-court strategy across 1965 to 1968, with Connor as Executive Secretary handling the precinct-and-county delegate work that fed the 1968 Mississippi Loyalist Democrats delegation that successfully replaced the regular Mississippi delegation at the Chicago Democratic National Convention [src: 4, src: 6]. The infrastructure that made the precinct-level work possible was the Forrest County NAACP under Vernon Dahmer Sr. and Clarence Magee (entry 21), with Dahmer's January 1966 firebombing assassination by the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan marking the cost the local organizing base paid for the registration work; the Hattiesburg African American Heritage Trail records and the Mississippi Department of Archives and History collection at Shady Grove Baptist Church document the funeral and the response of the local movement [src: 3, src: 5, src: 7].
legacy_and_reception: The Hattiesburg African American Heritage Trail, established in 2014 by the City of Hattiesburg and the Hattiesburg Convention Commission, includes 510 Mobile Street and the broader Mobile-Bouie Street corridor among its commemorated sites, with Connor named as one of the local organizers whose business anchored the 1964 Freedom Summer infrastructure [src: 5]. The 2014 Freedom Summer fiftieth-anniversary commemoration brought Connor and other surviving Hattiesburg organizers together at the University of Southern Mississippi, with the McCain Library and Archives' Civil Rights in Mississippi Digital Archive preserving the oral histories and ephemera from the commemoration alongside the Connor papers and the broader Hattiesburg COFO record [src: 5, src: 8]. Her Civil Rights History Project oral history was recorded by interviewer Emilye Crosby on November 30, 2015 at the McCain Library and Archives at the University of Southern Mississippi, with John Bishop and Guha Shankar on the recording team [src: 1, src: 9]. The COFO Hattiesburg infrastructure she helped anchor continued to operate through the 1968 Mississippi Loyalist delegation work and into the broader 1970s Mississippi voter-registration and local-government candidacy patterns that the MFDP precinct system seeded [src: 4, src: 6].

gallery.alt: Fannie Lou Hamer at the 1964 Democratic National Convention, Atlantic City, August 22, 1964
gallery.caption: Fannie Lou Hamer, MFDP delegate, photographed at the August 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, where Connor and the rest of the MFDP delegation refused the two-seat compromise the Johnson administration offered. Photo by Warren K. Leffler, U.S. News and World Report staff photographer, Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division, digital ID ds.07134.

gallery.caption: Aaron Henry, chair of the MFDP delegation, addressing the Credentials Committee at the August 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City. Connor was a member of the same delegation. Photo by Warren K. Leffler, U.S. News and World Report staff photographer, Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division, digital ID ppmsca.04299.

gallery.alt: Vernon Dahmer funeral, January 15, 1966, Shady Grove Baptist Church in Kelly Settlement, Hattiesburg
gallery.caption: The January 15, 1966 funeral service for Vernon Dahmer Sr. at Shady Grove Baptist Church in Kelly Settlement, Hattiesburg, after his firebombing assassination by the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan. Dahmer led the Forrest County NAACP that anchored the Hattiesburg COFO infrastructure Connor organized through. From the Mississippi Department of Archives and History collection, Flickr Commons, no known copyright restrictions.
